channel.title: Climate Risk (Google Alerts)
channel.description: Últimos artigos de Climate Risk (Google Alerts)

item.title: Midwest's Blowout Year of Storms, Power Outages Is a Window into Its Climate-Changed Future
item.description:
Weatherwise, 2026’s warm season has been one for the record books in many parts of the Midwest. A seemingly endless barrage of severe and, at times, deadly thunderstorms and tornados have wreaked havoc on homes and businesses, hundreds of thousands of which experienced power outages as a result.

As climate change is projected to increase severe thunderstorm activity across much of the Midwest, the power sector should heed this year’s pummeling as a wake-up call and reflect on whether it’s ready to withstand more of the same. This year offers an opportunity to imagine more concretely into a climate changed-world, to consider where system vulnerabilities lie, and where investments are needed to ensure our grid is resilient and able to reliably and equitably provide electricity for homes and businesses across the region. 

2026 weather extremes blow past 30-year averages

Taking stock of this year’s weather extremes in the Midwest is personal for me. And not to bury the lede: my family is fine, but we just barely avoided landing in the crosshairs of some of the most dangerous storms that have hit my home state of Wisconsin.

In mid-June, my husband and daughter were hunkered down in a barn in Belleville, Wisconsin, waiting out a severe thunderstorm that produced a tornado down the road. The storm destroyed two homes and severely damaged several more in Belleville. 

On another occasion, my daughter spent her last hour of the school year sheltered in place in her school’s basement as a severe thunderstorm roared through the area, producing 80+ MPH wind gusts. It was such a strong storm that it was eventually classified as a derecho— a particularly extreme class of severe thunderstorms. 

Looking out from the personal to the broader pattern, this has been an astounding year for Wisconsin. This April was the most tornadic on record in 76 years, with 27 tornados touching down across the state versus the more typical single April tornado. May was a relatively calm month, but there were 58 percent more severe storms in June than average. 

Wisconsin is certainly not alone. Most states in the Midwest had above average tornado counts in April. However, Illinois has been the hardest hit, experiencing the most tornados of any state in the US this year. Illinois has already seen 212 tornadoes form—more than three times its annual average of the last thirty years. To date, the National Weather Service has issued a whopping 812 severe thunderstorm warnings in Illinois.

State NWS Severe Thunderstorm Warnings NWS 30-Year Average Severe Thunderstorm Warnings (1996-2025) Tornados That Formed 30-Year Average Tornado Counts (1996-2025) Illinois 812 608 212 64 Wisconsin 474 347 46 25 Michigan 331 361 26 14

Consequences for our power system

The deadly and damaging severe storms that have hit the Midwest this year have been incredibly consequential for power outages, and things are not slowing down yet. On Independence Day weekend, deadly, climate change-driven extreme heat and severe thunderstorms drove prolonged power outages for hundreds of thousands of homes and businesses across the Midwest, leaving many in dangerous, hot conditions. 

Nearly two million homes and businesses lost power as a result of a series of severe thunderstorms that hit the Midwest and Ohio Valley in mid-June, according to an analysis by PowerOutage.us. Thousands of homes and businesses lost their power for multiple days on end as a result of the storms, including in my hometown of Madison, where the storms downed power lines and broke power poles. Check out this incredible simulation of the power outages caused by some of the severe thunderstorms rolling through on June 10. 

Unfortunately, this year is unlikely to be an anomaly with respect to severe storms causing power outages in the Midwest. Important aspects of severe thunderstorm activity are already strengthening as a result of human-caused climate change. 

In addition, warmer temperatures—and with them, thunderstorms—are expected to occur earlier in the Midwest as they did this spring, lengthening the timeframe within which severe thunderstorms can form. And climate change is forecast to increase the likelihood of extreme heat that can co-occur with severe storms and strain the power system as happened on Independence Day weekend, leaving communities in harm’s way.

It is therefore critical that power sector actors heed the warning that this year is giving them. As my colleagues and I recommend in our report Power After the Storm, detailed climate risk assessments must be carried out to understand what the grid is going to be up against and how risks should be mitigated and addressed. 

We also urge the power sector to transition to electricity grids powered by clean energy so that we can prevent the worst impacts of climate change from manifesting. Finally, we encourage engagement with communities in order that investments and decisions reflect the needs of all customers that our electricity grids are meant to serve.

By taking lived experiences from this year into account together with advances in climate science, we can make our electricity grid more resilient and ready to handle the more extreme weather that is headed our way.

The author would like to thank the Wisconsin State Climatology Office for their assistance with some of the statistics used in this blog.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: New USGCRP Director Could Reshape Climate Risk Baseline
item.description: A climate science critic now leads the USGCRP, the federal program behind the National Climate Assessment that underpins infrastructure risk ...
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Crown Holdings shares climate transition plan | Packaging Dive
item.description:
feedback.

Cans specialist Crown Holdings published a climate transition plan last month, which CEO Tim Donahue said covers the company’s “climate ambition,” including a decarbonization road map, approach to climate risks and other components.

Crown has numerous 2030 sustainability goals under a program it calls Twentyby30. Under that framework, the company aims for a 50% combined reduction in absolute scope 1 and scope 2 emissions, from a 2019 baseline. It also seeks to cut its scope 3 emissions — which account for 91% of its overall greenhouse gas emissions footprint, especially due to upstream purchased goods and services, transportation and distribution — by 27.5%.

By the end of 2025, Crown had nearly hit that goal, at 25%. Continued reduction strategies include collaborating with suppliers on sustainable sourcing and shared decarbonization efforts, and continuing to explore more efficient materials and solutions in its own products and secondary packaging.

These efforts have also necessitated better data. To improve accurate calculations, “we organized face-to-face meetings with the majority of our aluminum and steel suppliers, fostering dialogue and collaboration around emissions data,” the company reported. “These engagements enabled us to request recycled content information and supplier-specific emission factors.” This also promotes a shared responsibility approach, Crown noted.

Crown also detailed strategies to specifically target transportation emissions. These include increasing the use of intermodal transport, namely rail and sea options to mitigate emissions from long-distance road shipments. “In parallel, load optimization efforts are helping increase truck utilization and reduce the total number of trips required,” the company reported.

Long-term, Crown is seeking a 90% reduction in scope 3 emissions from purchased goods and services, upstream transportation and distribution and use of sold products by 2050.

The plan also notes climate risks on Crown’s radar. These include costs from rising energy prices, disruptions to raw material supply chains or possible physical damage to facilities from severe weather.

“We use climate scenario analysis to explore a range of future climate outcomes and inform long-term business planning,” Crown reported, noting the example of entering virtual power purchase agreements as a result of this analysis.

While climate transition plans are less common than annual sustainability reports, it’s a step that some other packaging suppliers, such as Ball, have taken.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Climate disclosure gives Canadian companies an edge with European investors, new ...
item.description:
Canadian companies that disclose their climate-related risks and impacts have a considerable advantage over those that don’t when it comes to attracting financing from European institutional investors, according to our recent report for the Institute for Sustainable Finance at Queen’s University.

That advantage matters now more than ever. Climate disclosure — companies publicly reporting on their greenhouse gas emissions, climate-related risks and how they plan to manage them — has become a harder sell.

Backlash against environmental, social and governance investing is gripping the United States and many corporate leaders have gone quiet on sustainability.

Canada’s stock market skews toward capital-intensive industries that rely heavily on foreign investment such as energy, materials, industrials and utilities. Remaining visible and attractive to major institutional investors is especially important for these companies, and Europe is the largest source of non-North American institutional investment in Canada, according to our data.

United States President Donald Trump’s tariffs and the broader unpredictability of American trade policy have pushed Canada to diversify its economic relationships away from reliance on the U.S. Attracting more capital from Europe gives Canadian companies a buffer against that volatility.

Climate disclosure is one of the clearest levers Canadian companies have to make themselves attractive to European capital. European investors increasingly need credible sustainability information to meet their own reporting obligations, and Canadian companies that lag on climate disclosure risk shutting themselves out of European capital markets altogether.

After Trump’s tariffs

Our report provides preliminary evidence of this European preference for climate-reporting firms. We examined whether climate disclosures helped Canadian firms attract foreign institutional investors following Trump’s April 2, 2025 announcement of sweeping global tariffs, which he dubbed “Liberation Day.”

We chose that announcement because it created a major external shock to the markets, leading many investors to reassess the risks associated with U.S. assets and prompting some international investors to reduce their exposure to that market.

Liberation Day offered a useful test: as capital moved away from the U.S., did firms that disclosed climate data attract more of it than firms that didn’t? The answer, we found, is yes.

After the shock, firms that reported climate data experienced an almost 25 per cent increase in foreign institutional holdings compared with firms that didn’t disclose. This effect was driven entirely by European investors. The result is statistically meaningful, but the significance level is relatively modest, so it should be interpreted as suggestive rather than conclusive evidence.

We ran additional robustness tests, statistical checks meant to rule out the most likely alternative explanations. We tested whether foreign institutional investors were avoiding Canadian firms that were more financially affected by the tariff announcement and whether they were avoiding Canadian firms that did more business in the U.S. In both cases, our results held.

Europe’s investors want climate data

Climate change is top of mind for institutional investors in Europe, which has the world’s most comprehensive sustainability regulatory environment. European investors understand how climate factors affect a company’s strategy, risk management and financial performance.

For investors operating in the European Union, sustainability performance can be just as important as financial metrics in making portfolio allocation decisions.

Under the EU’s sustainable finance rules, financial market participants are required to disclose sustainability indicators, such as greenhouse gas emissions, carbon footprint, biodiversity, water, waste and social factors. The data must be collected either directly from investee companies or through research that may include third-party data and experts.

When investing in jurisdictions that operate under a mostly voluntary reporting regime, like Canada, disclosure has unique value to European institutional investors because it helps them meet their own reporting obligations.

A case for mandatory disclosure in Canada

Our findings suggest disclosure practices are already shaping where European capital is invested in Canada. It’s likely other countries with voluntary reporting regimes are seeing similar patterns.

Our report also adds to a growing body of evidence in favour of stronger climate disclosure rules. The benefits include better-informed investors, clarity on climate-related risks to financial markets and stronger incentives for companies to act on emissions.

Several major capital hubs are already strengthening their sustainability disclosure regulations, including Japan, Singapore, Australia, Chile and Mexico. In the U.S., California and New York are among several states pressing ahead with their own emissions-reporting rules despite the federal pullback on climate policy.

Savvy Canadian companies have so far been able to retain the interest of European institutional capital through voluntary disclosure. But Canadian securities regulators have the opportunity to follow Europe’s example and mandate climate disclosures for larger Canadian public companies.

Climate change doesn’t care about whether sustainability is in fashion, and the risks are growing. Among other benefits, expanding disclosures could help keep Canadian firms competitive in international capital markets going forward.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Climate Adaptation Research and Innovation Programme Inventory - GOV.UK
item.description:
Climate Adaptation Research and Innovation Programme Inventory

Published 13 July 2026

This is not a statement of government policy.

1. Introduction

The Climate Change Committee’s recent evidence clearly showed how the UK is already experiencing the impacts of climate change – from more frequent and intense heatwaves to flooding, drought, biodiversity loss, and disruption to supply chains. These trends are projected to accelerate, with significant implications for public health, economic stability, national security, and social equity.

While mitigation efforts remain vital to limit future climate impacts, adaptation is essential to protect people, places, and the economy from risks that are already locked in. However, adaptation remains under-researched and under-invested in relative to mitigation. The current pace and scale of action is insufficient to meet the UK’s climate resilience needs.

Research and Innovation (R&I) can make it easier to mend this important shortfall by generating new knowledge, developing practical tools and technologies, identifying cost-effective solutions, and building adaptive capacity across institutions and communities.

The Climate Adaptation Research and Innovation Framework (CARIF) provides a non-exhaustive collation of adaptation R&I needs across sectors and ‘systems’, key to strengthening the UK’s climate resilience. These needs overlap with the areas in need of critical investigation identified in the CCC’s recent Well-Adapted UK report, such as: insufficient data on assets, vulnerabilities, and real-world climate impacts; weak evidence on the effectiveness of adaptation interventions over the long-term, and; gaps in economic analysis of damages and adaptation costs.

This inventory provides an overview of the main Government-funded R&I programmes that support climate adaptation, where adaptation is a core or significant component. It may not capture all relevant activity – some Government programmes include adaptation-related research as part of broader portfolios that may not be substantial enough to be included here. The Climate Adaptation Research and Innovation Board (CARIB) will continue to provide strategic oversight, monitoring and evaluation of progress against the CARIF Inventory.

As the programmes in this inventory are implemented, the UK government will be developing its fourth Climate Change Risk Assessment (CCRA4) and fourth National Adaptation Programme (NAP4), drawing on the recent advice, evidence and recommendations from the Climate Change Committee.

2. Climate adaptation R&I programmes funded by UK government departments

Programmes included within this inventory may span multiple funding periods, with some having commenced prior to 2026 and/or extending beyond 2030.

2.1. Health

Programme Programme Summary CARIF System Challenge Duration of Programme (Years) Total Funding (£m) FY25/26 F726/27 FY27/28 FY28/29 FY29/30 DHSC’s NIHR / UKRI Net Zero & Health Co-Benefits Hubs Seven transdisciplinary, place-based hubs examining health co-benefits and resilience implications of the net zero transition. £41.925m overall spend for FY24/25 to FY29/30 which consists of £23.9 m from NIHR and £18.5m from UKRI. Spatial analysis and a place-based approach 5 23.9 8.385 8.385 8.385 6.289 - DHSC’s NIHR Climate-Resilient Health & Social Care Research Research to adapt health and social care systems and infrastructure to climate-related extreme weather Early warning systems and tailored climate services 5 10.5 0.35 0.4 3.25 3.25 3.25 Defra’s Air Quality Research Projects These projects explore i) what impacts the changing climate and weather patterns will have on air pollutant concentrations and related health impacts in the UK ii) the relationship with interacting environmental factors (e.g. heat and pollen) iii) develop improved air pollution forecasts. Harnessing data for well‑designed and implemented adaptation strategies 4 0.875 - 0.275 0.2 0.2 0.2

2.2. Built environment and communities

Programme Programme Summary CARIF System Challenge Duration of Programme (Years) Total Funding (£m) FY25/26 F726/27 FY27/28 FY28/29 FY29/30 DESNZ’s Built Environment Adaptation Research (BEAR) Evidence base for climate-resilient UK building stock, including retrofit pathways, metrics and adaptation impacts Spatial analysis and a place-based approach 4 3.13 1.48 0.82 0.53 0.3 - Met Office’s Local Authority Climate Service (LACS) and Climate Data Portal Provides a place-based climate service supporting local authority adaptation planning and risk assessment, delivering accessible, locally tailored climate projections and indicators through the Met Office Climate Data Portal. Translates national climate science into decision-ready information for local planning, resilience, and statutory reporting, aligned with UKCP and national adaptation guidance. Spatial analysis and a place-based approach 5 0.575 0.175 0.1 0.1 0.1 0.1 Defra’s Floods Research Programme Programme comprising Defra-commissioned flood research and projects aimed at reducing and mitigating flood risk to properties, businesses and communities in England and Wales. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 5 2.61 0.45 0.55 0.61 0.50 0.50 Natural England’s proposed urban green infrastructure project In collaboration with Kew Gardens, this project aims to understand how we can increase nature and climate resilience through changes in management of green space. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 1 FTE 0.1 FTE 0.1 - - - -

2.3. Transport

Programme Programme Summary CARIF System Challenge Duration of Programme (Years) Total Funding (£m) FY25/26 F726/27 FY27/28 FY28/29 FY29/30 DfT’s Resilience Science Programme This programme is funding research on nature-based solutions for transport resilience, transport system interdependencies and whole-system resilience to inform transport adaptation Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies Ongoing 0.48 + TBC 0.12 0.36 TBC TBC TBC DfT’s Environment & Adaptation Division Research on understanding and measuring the impacts of warming on the transport system. Making the business case Ongoing 0.36 + TBC 0.18 0.18 TBC TBC TBC DfT’s Delivering Efficiency through Technology Adoption (DETA) Fund Climate resilience is 1 of 7 programmes of the DETA fund which will accelerate the development and adoption of new technology that can be applied across the transport sector. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 5 0.65 0.13 0.13 0.13 0.13 0.13 National Hub for Decarbonised, Adaptable, and Resilient Transport Infrastructures (DARe Hub) DfT and UKRI co-funded hub which will provide decision-makers – at local, regional and national levels – with transferable, open-source resources to evaluate infrastructure performance and stress-test adaptation options across future scenarios. Making the business case. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 3.5 10 2.5 2.5 - - -

2.4. Public services

Programme Programme Summary CARIF System Challenge Duration of Programme (Years) Total Funding (£m) FY25/26 F726/27 FY27/28 FY28/29 FY29/30 Defra’s Strategic Behavioural insights research for emergencies, net zero and climate resilience and preparedness Behavioural research programme focussing on strategic, cross-cutting areas in relation to emergencies, and climate mitigation and adaptation. Includes exploring behaviours relating to societal resilience including preparedness, response and recovery. Aims to improve preparedness, resilience and recovery outcomes, including climate adaptation and mitigation. Utilises existing and innovative methods and technologies, including AI-assisted research, to understand behavioural barriers and enablers and effectiveness of interventions. Research focus includes at risk groups. Understanding social, behavioural and governance barriers to adaptation and impacts of adaptation solutions 4 0.55 - 0.14 0.1 0.15 0.15

2.5. Water and wastewater

Programme Programme Summary CARIF System Challenge Duration of Programme (Years) Total Funding (£m) FY25/26 F726/27 FY27/28 FY28/29 FY29/30 Defra’s Water Research Programme Water research within the programme supports the outcomes of the Independent Water Commission and Environment Act targets by strengthening evidence, monitoring, and innovation across the water system. It includes research on emerging risks such as antimicrobial resistance in aquatic environments and microbiological hazards in recreational waters; advances in laboratory methods and data automation to improve efficiency and reduce costs; and evaluation of the Water Restoration Fund. The programme also develops the future water regulatory framework, including new monitoring regimes and tools to better target investment and track progress, alongside research to reduce agricultural pollution (notably nitrogen and phosphorus) while maintaining productivity and meeting statutory reduction targets. Harnessing data for well‑designed and implemented adaptation strategies 5 12.22 1.54 2.46 2.76 3.03 2.43 Defra’s funding of the Environment Agency’s Floods and Water (F&W) Programme The programme of research carried out by the EA in support of the Floods and Water Directorate. Projects inform regulatory duties including: 1) achieving a sustainable transition to net zero, 2) understanding and managing critical thresholds for Anti-Microbial Resistance (AMR) in surface waters, 3) water demand management, 4) resilient water resources and management of drought risk, 5) public attitudes to climate adaptation and water scarcity and 6) water requirements for net zero technology and land use change. Harnessing data for well‑designed and implemented adaptation strategies 5 15.16 2.87 3.2 (£0.5m for EA’s Climate Adaptation & Resilience Programme) 3.01 3.02 3.06 Defra / Environment Agency Climate Adaptation and Resilience Programme This programme aims to deliver targeted adaptation research to strengthen understanding of climate impacts on water systems and ecosystems and inform effective responses. It focuses on evaluating nature-based solutions, assessing risks such as drought, invasive species, changing catchments, and novel ecosystems, and improving evidence on pressures in rivers, estuaries, and lakes, including drying, algal blooms, and temperature change. Setting coherent adaptation goals and resilience standards, and monitoring progress 5 0.9 (EA core funding, £1.9m in total including Defra F&W funding) 0.45 (programme total is £0.95m with £0.5m from Defra F&W as above) 0.45 (programme total is £0.95m with £0.5m from Defra F&W as above) TBC TBC TBC

2.6. Energy

Programme Programme Summary CARIF System Challenge Duration of Programme (Years) Total Funding (£m) FY25/26 F726/27 FY27/28 FY28/29 FY29/30 DESNZ’s Climate Services for a Net Zero Resilient World (CS-N0W2) Climate risk evidence, resilience metrics and data services to support adaptation of energy and infrastructure systems Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 4 6 1.4 2.1 1.5 1 - DESNZ’s Energy Security & Resilience Research Evidence on climate risks, preparedness and resilience costs for the UK energy system Making the business case 4 TBC 0.2 TBC TBC TBC -

2.7. Land

Programme Programme Summary CARIF System Challenge Duration of Programme (Years) Total Funding (£m) FY25/26 F726/27 FY27/28 FY28/29 FY29/30 Scottish Government: Environment, Natural Resources and Agriculture (ENRA) Research Portfolio The Environment, Natural Resources and Agriculture (ENRA) Research Portfolio is a large-scale, multidisciplinary package that delivers research priorities pivotal in shaping the Scottish Government’s evidence-based responses to the challenges of climate change and biodiversity loss. The Scottish Government invests approx £45 million a year into a portfolio of strategic research to ensure that Scotland maintains its position at the very cutting edge of advances in agriculture, natural resources and the environment. Within the current strategic research programme, the Scottish Government has projects which support climate change adaptation. Harnessing data for well‑designed and implemented adaptation strategies 2022-2027 ENRA RP concludes in March 2027. The ENRA RP 2027-2032 five year programme starts in April 2027. TBC 12.4 12.4 TBC TBC TBC Defra’s Future Proofing Plant Health (FPPH) Active since 2014, FPPH aims to consolidate plant health research and development requirements delivered by Defra network organisations and encourage greater collaboration across the department’s core science providers. Fera Science, FR, Kew, Natural England & JNCC with further subcontracting to other providers. Annual call for research via a 5-year MOU. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 5 15.4 4.2 2.8 2.8 2.8 2.8 Defra’s Centre for Forest Protection (CFP) The CFP is a virtual research hub, led by Forest Research and RBG Kew in collaboration with a range of research providers and sector stakeholders. It brings together expertise and facilities to protect and enhance the future health and resilience of our forests, woodlands and trees. Jointly funded with NETL. 3-year programme of work via an MOU. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 3 (with programme likely to be extended) 12 (for phase 2 of programme) 4.0 4.0 4.0 TBC TBC Defra’s Euphresco Euphresco is a network of organisations that fund research projects and coordinate national and transnational research in the phytosanitary area including climate-driven adaption of pest and diseases. The overall goal of Euphresco is to support coordination and collaboration in the area of phytosanitary research, and to maintain itself as a strong, long-term global network of research stakeholders (Defra funds only UK research organisations). Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 5 1.3 0.25 0.25 0.30 0.30 0.30 Defra’s Climate Change effects on soil How resilient England’s soils are to future climate is underexplored and therefore how exposed current land use, such as agriculture, is, which is dependant on the provision of soil delivered ecosystems services. Spatial analysis and a place-based approach 4 0.8 0 0.2 0.2 0.2 0.2 Defra’s Trees, Woodlands and Forestry Co-funds forestry resilience and innovation research programmes, including the Centre for Forest Protection, responsive forestry R&D, and EU Horizon forest research, supporting future forest resilience, policy development and regulation. Harnessing data for well‑designed and implemented adaptation strategies 5 34.30 9.16 6.27 6.07 6.36 6.43 Defra’s National Biodiversity Evidence and Analysis Team (NBEAT) Programme aims to improve the spatial targeting and value for money of intervention delivery, while strengthening the evidence base underpinning statutory biodiversity targets and Environmental Improvement Plan (EIP) reporting to reduce legal and reputational risk. It will provide trajectories, monitoring, and evaluation data for key actions, enabling policymakers to track progress and adapt delivery where needed. Collectively, this will increase confidence in achieving biodiversity targets, leading to improved outcomes for species and habitats and delivering wider benefits for ecosystem services, including pollination, recreation, human health, and resilience. The evidence will enhance our understanding of improving connectivity of habitats and reducing pressure from pollution – all of which are important to help species adapt to climate change. Setting coherent adaptation goals and resilience standards, and monitoring progress 5 14.01 2.95 2.62 3.11 2.88 2.45 Defra’s Science and Innovation Strategy for Forestry in Britan. Cross-border forestry research programme supporting forestry authorities in England, Scotland and Wales through the Science and Innovation Strategy for Forestry in Britain, including research on climate adaptation, mitigation and long-term forest resilience. The strategy is being refreshed for 2027-2032 and funding figures include contributions from Scotland and Wales. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 5 45.35 9.07 9.07 9.07 9.07 9.07 Defra’s Access Landscapes Peats and Soils Research programme investigating peatland restoration, soil resilience and soil health, including greenhouse gas inventory improvements, enhanced spatial soils data. Harnessing data for well‑designed and implemented adaptation strategies 5 19 9.3 6.1 3.2 0.2 0.2 Defra and UKRI’s Resilient Economy and Security through Ecosystem Transitions Planned collaboration with UKRI to establish a large collaborative programme exploring the ways in which ecosystems may reorganise as a result of climate change, land use change and other anthropogenic and environmental pressures. This would explore changes in ecosystem function as species become locally extinct or migrate. It would explore cascading risks, e.g. to human security. It would also look at ways of managing ecosystems to enhance resilience, e.g. by retain key functional groups and increasing functional redundancy. Harnessing data for well‑designed and implemented adaptation strategies 3 21 - - TBC TBC TBC Defra’s Advancing biodiversity monitoring for global nature Programme strengthens biodiversity and environmental monitoring in lower- and middle-income countries through equitable co-design with Indigenous Peoples and local communities, improved data infrastructure, and open-access marine risk systems. It supports national planning, climate resilience, and global nature and climate targets by enhancing locally relevant monitoring, integrating Indigenous knowledge, building capacity, and exploring how novel monitoring approaches can support nature finance. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 2 (with programme likely to be extended) 0.99 0.34 0.65 TBC TBC TBC Defra’s Natural Capital and Ecosystem Assessment (NCEA) – Includes Core R&D Funding and funding from ELM & Defra Soils The NCEA programme is the UK’s largest ever ecological survey over a 5‑year baseline to provide robust, regionally representative data on ecosystem condition, supported by remote sensing, mapping, and soil (including mycorrhizal) research. This evidence base is enabling a natural capital approach to decision-making in government, as well as the wider public and private sectors. NCEA is contributing to adaptation by providing a foundation for understanding future change and informing long-term climate adaptation planning. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 5 183.31 63.24 56.28 53.09 10 0.7 Natural England’s Environment Improvement Plan (EIP) Action Research on increasing the natural resilience of habitats to wildfires, such as through re-wetting or restoring hydrological function, including case studies of success, by 2030. An initial scoping review will be reported on in the annual progress report (APR) in 2027. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 1 FTE 0.5 FTE 0.5 - - - - Natural England’s publication and report updates Natural England is updating its evidence base on climate impacts and adaptation for habitats and species through three core strands of work: (1) publication of version 3.0 of the Natural England Adaptation Manual, including revised habitat guidance; (2) a review and update of the 2016 Risks and Opportunities to Species report; and (3) updated outputs from the National Habitat Climate Change Vulnerability Model for all Priority Habitats. Alongside these updates, they are strengthening climate-informed species modelling, embedding climatic niche modelling across Natural England’s Data Science Services and applying it to priority species, and developing tools such as spatial prioritisation for the Nature Restoration Fund to incorporate future climate suitability and connectivity. Together, this work provides a more robust and up-to-date synthesis of climate risks, impacts, and adaptation responses for habitats and species, supporting more climate-resilient conservation and restoration decisions. Spatial analysis and a place-based approach 2 FTE 3 FTE 1.5 FTE 1.5 - - - Natural England’s exploration of climate driven inevitable change to habitats (PSS-funded R&D with UKCEH) Research includes calibrated climate vegetation models that will be used to explore potential tipping points in habitat composition and function. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 2 0.08 (TBC) + FTE 0.5 0.08 (TBC) + FTE 0.3 FTE 0.2 - - - Elements of Natural England’s Nature Returns Programme Investigation of the effects of weather on wetland mosaic greenhouse gas emissions (small contribution) Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 1 0.01 + FTE 0.3 0.01 + FTE 0.3 - - - - Natural England’s proposed integrated climate and natural capital risk for adaptive landscape management project This project will test how climate risk evidence and natural capital approaches can be integrated to support adaptive management planning at the landscape scale. It will assess how climate change pressures interact with ecosystem condition and function, with a particular focus on protected sites. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 2 FTE 1.4 FTE 0.7 FTE 0.7 - - - Natural England’s Climate Change Adaptation Monitoring for Environmental Land Management Schemes This project aims to develop a robust monitoring and evaluation (M&E) framework for assessing how Environmental Land Management (ELM) schemes (Sustainable Farming Incentive (SFI), Countryside Stewardship Higher Tier (CSHT) and Landscape Recovery (LR)) contribute to climate change adaptation, including responding to hazards such as flooding, drought, wildfire, extreme heat, storms, and coastal erosion. Setting coherent adaptation goals and resilience standards, and monitoring progress 2 0.1 + FTE 0.6 0.1 + FTE 0.3 FTE 0.3 - - - Natural England – creating and embedding adaptation indicators The project will develop a framework to assess and monitor the state and progress of adaptation at the landscape scale. Such a framework will enable an evidence-based consistent assessment of the ambition and effectiveness of delivery mechanisms such as Local Nature Recovery Strategies (LNRS), Landscape Recovery (LR) and Protected Sites Strategies (PSS) in delivering adaptation Setting coherent adaptation goals and resilience standards, and monitoring progress 2 FTE 1 FTE 0.5 FTE 0.5 - - - Animal & Plant Health Agency (APHA) – EU Partnership on Animal Health and Welfare (EUPAHW) Aims to provide future wild mammal distribution and abundance changes in UK and across Europe, based on climatic and habitat change to help establish early warning disease surveillance systems and improved contingency planning. This will also include potential changes in invasive species risks. Co-funded with Defra and European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) ENETwild Spatial analysis and a place-based approach 3 (with programme likely to be extended) 0.26 - 0.07 (EU contribution 48%) TBC TBC - APHA Project: Rift Valley fever virus in temperate mosquitoes: investigating persistence and enhancing outbreak preparedness capabilities. This Defra funded project underpins APHA’s National Reference Laboratory (NRL) for Rift Vally fever virus (RVFV) as well as investigating the competence of British vector species to harbour the virus. This project sits within the Animal and Zoonotic Viral Disease Portfolio. Spatial analysis and a place-based approach 4 1.19 - 0.32 0.31 0.28 0.28 APHA Project: Elucidating cryptic transmission pathways for zoonotic flaviviruses to predict outbreaks of notifiable disease This Defra-funded project focuses on two objectives. Firstly, using British Trust Ornithology (BTO) ring recovery data to map bird movements and assess how migratory birds may introduce and spread mosquito-borne viruses in the UK. Secondly, sampling Blackbird nests to investigate their role in the epidemiology of Usutu virus (USUV), particularly during the sedentary nestling stage when mosquito transmission may occur. Together, this addresses a key policy gap by identifying high-risk areas and species, while supporting improved disease modelling and risk assessment. Spatial analysis and a place-based approach 3 0.2 - 0.07 0.07 0.06 - Natural England – Species Risks and OpportunitiesThe R&D project will conduct a review of the predictions of the NECR0175 on the Risks & Opportunities to Species. This report represents a significant evidence base for understanding the impact of climate change on species. A further 10 years of observations and impacts are now available to determine whether the outputs of the work remain valid. Dependent on the results of the review, further work may be required. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies20.8 + FTE 0.60.8 + FTE 0.3FTE 0.3---

2.8. Sea

Programme Programme Summary CARIF System Challenge Duration of Programme (Years) Total Funding (£m) FY25/26 F726/27 FY27/28 FY28/29 FY29/30 Defra’s Aquatic animal health – core R&D Underpins research to support Defra policy and Centre for Environment, Fisheries and Aquaculture Science (Cefas) core research and diagnostic capability in aquatic animal disease, supporting the development of innovative diagnostic technologies, better awareness of disease risks and increased sensitivity of early warning systems. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 3 2.85 - 0.97 0.94 0.94 - Defra’s Aquatic animal health – evidence R&D Supports the health of the UK’s aquatic animals and the resilience of the fish farming sector by addressing targeted knowledge and evidence gaps in relevant aquatic animal disease. Closely aligned with national capability and core-funded research programme at Cefas. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 5 1.12 0.20 0.22 0.22 0.23 0.24 Defra’s Aquatic animal health – official services Provides inspection, surveillance, diagnostic and scientific capability for the control of serious aquatic animal diseases in England and Wales, including epidemiological risk assessment, veterinary advice, emerging disease investigation, and operation of national and international reference laboratories. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 1 - 6.35 - - - - Defra’s Managing Pressures in our Marine Environment This programme aims to deliver evidence to enable strong evidence is available to inform decisions on issues including spatial squeeze, marine protection and how we can manage multiple pressures on our marine environment. This work will enable Defra to identify adaption strategies where climate changes pose a risk. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 4 17.2 - 4.5 4.5 4.1 4.1 Defra’s Marine & Fisheries Risk and Horizon Scanning Research aimed to increase understanding of how the environment is changing with risk emerging which may require future policy response including the need for adaptation Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 5 12.1 - 3.1 3 3 3 NI Executive – PEACEPLUS PEACEPLUS is a cross-border funding programme supported by the European Union, the Government of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland, the Government of Ireland, and the Northern Ireland administration. Multi-disciplinary Ocean Sensing for Adaptive International Conservation (MOSAIC) – NI Executive contribution of £6.9m (€8 m equivalent). Coastal Monitoring and Adaptation Planning (CMAP) – NI Executive contribution of £8.3m (€9.6 m equivalent) Setting coherent adaptation goals and resilience standards, and monitoring progress 4 15.2 - - - - - NI Executive – Northern Ireland Coastal Forum. Co-chaired by Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA), and Department for Infrastructure Ministers. NIEA, GSNI, National Trust, AFBI, academia, UK Hydrographic Office, local authorities working to build on the scientifically robust evidence base of coastal data, repeating high resolution surveys to identify coastal change and undertake coastal vulnerability assessments based on the evidence collected. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies Ongoing - - - - - -

2.9. Food security

Programme Programme Summary CARIF System Challenge Duration of Programme (Years) Total Funding (£m) FY25/26 F726/27 FY27/28 FY28/29 FY29/30 Defra / Met Office Partnership – Food, Farming and Natural Environment Climate Services (FFNE) Defra funds the Met Office Food, Farming & Natural Environment (FFNE) climate service, which provides essential and world-leading climate modelling services to Defra. The climate service provides a sector-focused climate service supporting agriculture, food systems and the natural environment, delivering targeted analysis and evidence to inform policy development on food security, land use, pests and diseases, and ecosystem resilience. It connects UK climate projections to sector-specific impacts and adaptation needs, supporting statutory processes including the Climate Change Risk Assessment, National Adaptation Programme and UK Food Security Report. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 4 1.39 0.27 0.36 0.37 0.39 - Defra’s Crop Genetics and Breeding Research A programme of breeding research (Genetic Improvement Networks) to improve the productivity, resilience, sustainability and nutritional quality of key UK crops. Adapting UK crops to a changing climate is a key focus of this research. The crop Genetic Improvement Networks (GINs) deliver collaborative lab-based R&D supported by field trials to test out breeding benefits for key UK crops. This research helps today’s crops to grow stronger, be more resilient to pests, diseases and a changing climate with efficient inputs. Harnessing data for well‑designed and implemented adaptation strategies 5 13.70 3.96 2.67 3.45 2.83 0.86

2.10. Economy and finance

Programme Programme Summary CARIF System Challenge Duration of Programme (Years) Total Funding (£m) FY25/26 F726/27 FY27/28 FY28/29 FY29/30 Defra’s research for Circular Policy-Making Supports the development of a new, ambitious strategic evidence-gathering programme to understand the citizen and business transition to a circular economy. Through a bespoke citizen survey, collaborating with ONS to adapt and expand business surveys, and testing and trialling behavioural insights interventions, the programme will enhance understanding of the barriers to adopting more circular and therefore adaptive behaviours and practices. Understanding social, behavioural and governance barriers to adaptation and impacts of adaptation solutions 4 1.9 - 0.3 0.3 0.6 0.6

2.11. National security and international engagement

Programme Programme Summary CARIF System Challenge Duration of Programme (Years) Total Funding (£m) FY25/26 F726/27 FY27/28 FY28/29 FY29/30 FCDO’s CLARE – Framework on Climate Adaptation & Resilience Southern-led adaptation research and innovation in Africa and Asia-Pacific, bridging science and policy Spatial analysis and a place-based approach 2 84.5 18 13.6 TBC TBC TBC FCDO’s UNEP Climate-Resilient Finance Partnership Provides support to finance ministries in developing countries to embed climate resilience into fiscal and economic planning Making the business case 1 3.5 3.5 - - - - Defra’s Nature Security The Nature Security research programme explores how global ecosystem degradation poses risk to UK national security. Within this R&D area, there is a planned three-year research programme to build the analytical frameworks, tools and early warning capability that enable HMG to understand, anticipate and respond to the national security risks arising from global ecosystem degradation and change. Alongside this sit 2 shorter-term pieces of work: 1 scoping study exploring UK national security risks relating from freshwater supply globally, and another to understand potential UK security risks from UK and EU ecosystem degradation. Early warning systems and tailored climate services 3 TBC - - TBC TBC TBC Defra’s Biodiversity Research and Insights for Delivery under Global Environmental Frameworks (BRIDGE) This programme explores the UK’s role in shaping global biodiversity goals beyond 2030. It assesses ambition levels, scenario planning, and while evaluating IPBES influence, cross-sector collaboration, and will use biodiversity modelling approaches. This programme will provide decision-ready, independent evidence that connects biodiversity ambition and international commitments with real-world delivery at home and abroad. BRIDGE will strengthen the evidence base on restoring and protecting biodiversity and ecosystems to enhance resilience and identifying gaps and recommending forward-looking actions and pathways, while informing future global target setting, thereby supporting nature-based climate adaptation. It aims to strengthen UK contributions to global frameworks through improved monitoring, integration, and evidence-based international engagement. Setting coherent adaptation goals and resilience standards, and monitoring progress 4 TBC - TBC TBC TBC TBC Defra’s Global Centre on Biodiversity for Climate (GCBC) Funds research in ODA-eligible countries on how biodiversity conservation and the sustainable use of nature can address climate change, strengthen resilience and support livelihoods. Making the business case 3 30 10 10 10 - - FCDO’s SCALE – System-level Climate Adaptation LEvers Research programme Research and innovation to build climate resilience at scale for the most vulnerable in the Global South, focusing on systemic resilience, catalysing action, climate science, adaptation finance, and climate-related mobility. Understanding social, behavioural and governance barriers to adaptation and impacts of adaptation solutions 6 39.8 - 0.4 8.7 9.3 9

2.12. Multi-sectoral

Programme Programme Summary CARIF System Challenge Duration of Programme (Years) Total Funding (£m) FY25/26 F726/27 FY27/28 FY28/29 FY29/30 Defra’s Programme for Adaptation & Climate Evidence (PACE) This programme aims to deliver a coherent, system-wide evidence base to inform UK climate adaptation policy and support cross-government decision-making on climate risks. It includes delivering evidence for the UK Climate Change Risk Assessment (CCRA), driving scientific excellence with the Met Office through UK Climate Information (UKCI), supporting evidence-to-policy application via the Maximising UK Adaptation to Climate Change (MACC) Hub (co-funded with UKRI as seen in Section 2.12 below), and developing best practice through a new What Works Centre for Adaptation. Collectively, these activities provide a national, evidence-based assessment of cross-government, sectoral, regional and systemic climate risks and enable the development of effective adaptation policy. Setting coherent adaptation goals and resilience standards, and monitoring progress 5 34.54 5.92 3.42 4 7.7 13.5 NI Executive – DAERA. DAERA Directed AFBI Research Work Programme (DDARWP) Annual programme to commission and oversee R&D from the Agri-Food Biosciences Institute, an arm’s-length body of the Department to help deliver evidence and new innovations to assist in the delivery of a pathway to net zero and underpin resilient sectors through resource-efficient and sufficient circular economies which are resilient and adapted to the current and projected effects of climate change, as well as providing good green jobs for globally competitive and resilient industries. There are 3 current adaptation-specific projects. Part of an ongoing DAERA Directed Research Programme across 5 thematic areas, including climate, with an annual expenditure of £10–12m. The figure presented reflects adaptation-specific programmes only. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies Annual durations of adaptation programmes within span 2-3 years 1.155 0.182 0.653 0.320 - - NI Executive – DAERA. The Co-Centre Programme. Climate Plus Biodiversity Plus Water Co-Centre The Co-Centre Programme is a new strategic collaborative partnership between Ireland, Great Britain and Northern Ireland, co-funded by industry, which will consolidate research activities across higher education and research institutes in co-hosted centres. The programme will support large-scale, virtual centres with the aim of strengthening the research and innovation base within Ireland, Great Britain and Northern Ireland, building strategic partnerships across these regions to support a competitive economy and addressing societal and environmental challenges. Currently, there are four Climate Plus Biodiversity Plus Water Co-Centre adaption specific projects. Climate Plus Biodiversity Plus Water Co-Centre is co-funded by UKRI and Research Ireland. The figure presented reflects investment by DAERA. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 6 8.4 - - - - - NI Executive – DAERA. DAERA Postgraduate Studentship Scheme DAERA funds full-time postgraduate studentships for a three-year period of research to address policy priorities leading to a PhD qualification at a university in the United Kingdom or Republic of Ireland. There are 5 climate adaptation related projects. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies Annual programme. Individual postgraduate studentships last 3 years 0.434 TBC TBC TBC TBC TBC Defra’s Advancing wetland science for climate, people and policy Multi-year programme supporting UK implementation of the Ramsar Convention on Wetlands through development of a UK Wetland Inventory and research on climate resilience, wetland carbon, nature-based solutions, governance, policy effectiveness and monitoring technologies to inform UK policy, international collaboration and future research priorities. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 5 1.34 0.33 0.46 0.35 0.35 0.2 Defra / Met Office’s UK Climate Information (UKCI) Funded by Defra and delivered by the Met Office, UKCI provides next-generation UK climate information, advancing climate and impacts science to deliver decision-relevant projections, metrics and tools. Develops user-focused platforms and guidance to ensure climate information is accessible, usable and applied in real-world decision-making. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 4 12 2 4 4 2 - Met Office’s Hadley Centre Programme (HCCP) Funded by UK government departments and delivered by the Met Office Hadley Centre, HCCP develops and maintains critical infrastructure such as climate models, observations, and datasets, and directly supports key national products including the UK Climate Projections (UKCP) and the Climate Change Risk Assessments (CCRA). Its primary role is upstream: ensuring the UK has robust, credible, and policy-relevant climate science to inform mitigation, adaptation, and resilience planning across government and beyond. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 5 121.8 22.6 22.9 24.4 25.4 26.4 Natural England’s assessment of Adaptive Delivery Plans (ADP)s/ R(esist)-A(ccept)-D(irect). (RAD) Framework This work will complete the testing and trialling of ADPs on terrestrial SSSIs to include consideration of appropriate monitoring & evaluation. It will also explore the application of the ADP process into marine protected areas (MPAs). Setting coherent adaptation goals and resilience standards, and monitoring progress 2 FTE 1 FTE 0.5 FTE 0.5 - - -

3. UKRI-funded projects and programmes

UKRI expects to continue to fund a broad range of adaptation and resilience activity through ongoing schemes, including for example curiosity-driven research, the work of relevant centres and institutes, international partnerships and investments in talent and skills. UKRI funding is primarily drawn from non-departmental sources.

Programme Programme Summary CARIF System Challenge Duration of Programme (Years) Total Funding (£m) FY25/26 F726/27 FY27/28 FY28/29 FY29/30 Building resilience to wildfire in a changing climate A tri-lateral partnership to improve understanding of wildfire occurrence, intensity, behaviour and spread within the UK, the US, and Canada. Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 4 7 - 1 2 2 2 Future Observatory: Design the Green Transition Connecting designers, researchers, and communities to support the shift to a sustainable, low‑carbon future Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 3 34.4 2.6 5.95 6.85 - - UKRI-NSFC Sustainable Efficiencies in Urban Environments Programme Part of the UK-China Flagship challenge focused on sustainable cities Place-based adaptation and spatial decision-making 4 8 - 0.1 1.5 2.5 2.4 Floods and Droughts Research Infrastructure A ‘digital accelerator’ for research into the drivers, scope and impact of severe weather on the UK and enhance AI-based predictive capability Harnessing data for well-designed and implemented adaptation strategies 10 40 - - - - - Defra and UKRI – Maximising UK Adaptation to Climate Change (MACC) Hub Cross-government hub providing decision support, evidence synthesis, guidance and coordination to support effective adaptation action, co-funded by Defra and UKRI. Setting coherent adaptation goals and resilience standards, and monitoring progress 3 7.5 3.7 3.7 0.1 - -

item.title: How Efficiently Did the Market Price Vale's Stock After the Mariana and Brumadinho Disasters?
